title: Set Up Deterministic Construction And Evaluation
description: Create one reproducible environment for benchmark construction, local-model inference, scoring, and statistical analysis that fits 1x RTX A6000 48GB, 60GB RAM, 4 CPU cores, and an 8-hour total experiment budget.

steps.step1: Create a Python 3.11 environment and install pinned packages: torch==2.5.*, vllm==0.6.*, transformers==4.46.*, accelerate==1.*, datasets==3.*, pandas==2.*, numpy==2.*, scipy==1.14.*, scikit-learn==1.5.*, statsmodels==0.14.*, matplotlib==3.9.*, seaborn==0.13.*, krippendorff==0.7.*, jsonlines, pydantic, sentencepiece, rapidfuzz, and sqlglot.
steps.step2: Write `artifacts/run_manifest.json` with GPU model, CUDA version, driver version, CPU core count, RAM, Python version, package versions, git commit hash, wall-clock timestamps, and the three pre-registered random seeds [11, 22, 33].
steps.step3: Use one shared inference wrapper for every model and metric: temperature=0.0, top_p=1.0, do_sample=false, max_new_tokens=32, repetition_penalty=1.0, and an answer-only prompt suffix such as `Return only the final answer.`
steps.step4: Run models in BF16 when they fit under 44GB VRAM. If needed, load the largest model in 4-bit quantization and keep all other models in BF16. Keep only one model on the GPU at a time because there is a single GPU.
steps.step5: Parallelize CPU-only jobs over the 4 available cores while GPU inference is running: normalization checks, bootstrap resampling, inter-annotator agreement computation, split-wise analyses, and figure rendering.
steps.step6: Save all outputs in fixed formats: candidates as JSONL, accepted clusters as JSON, annotation decisions as CSV, model predictions as JSONL, metrics as CSV, confidence intervals as JSON, and figures as both PNG and PDF.

title: Freeze Benchmark Scope And Evaluation Claims
description: Pre-register the benchmark as an evaluation-quality and diagnostic-failure-analysis paper, not a novelty-first leaderboard paper, so the experiments test benchmark validity and coupled scoring rather than overclaiming rank changes.

steps.step1: Freeze the release unit as a verified 4-item semantic cluster q0-q3, where q0 is the seed, q1 and q2 are answer-preserving paraphrases, and q3 is one minimal answer-changing flip with a deterministically recomputed gold answer. Define q4 as a format-control item that is always scored separately.
steps.step2: Freeze the target release at 96 clusters if all slices pass: 28 arithmetic or symbolic, 24 temporal, 20 table or list, and up to 24 evidence-grounded. Freeze the guaranteed fallback release at 72 procedural-core clusters if the evidence-grounded slice fails its gates.
steps.step3: Create two disjoint construction splits before model evaluation: Split A and Split B. Under the 96-cluster plan, assign 48 accepted clusters to each split with balanced family composition. Under the 72-cluster fallback plan, assign 36 accepted clusters to each split with the same balancing rule.
steps.step4: Record per-cluster metadata fields: cluster_id, construction_split, family, source_type, source_date, provenance_url_or_template_id, q0-q4 text, gold answers for q0-q4, normalization rule, flip template, solver or extractor type, generation seed, annotator decisions, adjudication outcome, and final keep or drop reason.
steps.step5: Pre-register the main paper claim in falsifiable form: q0 Seed Accuracy will systematically overestimate coupled semantic robustness, measured by a non-trivial drop from q0 Seed Accuracy to Cluster Semantic Score, and this gap should replicate across both construction splits.
steps.step6: Pre-register that any model-rank changes between q0 accuracy and Cluster Semantic Score are exploratory unless bootstrap confidence intervals and a minimum-detectable-effect analysis show enough power to separate the compared systems.

title: Generate Candidate Pools For Four Benchmark Families
description: Construct a candidate pool large enough to survive filtering and dual human verification while keeping annotation time compatible with a small, auditable benchmark release.

steps.step1: Generate approximately 144 candidate clusters total to target a 96-cluster release or a 72-cluster fallback core. Aim for 40 arithmetic or symbolic candidates to keep 28, 34 temporal to keep 24, 30 table or list to keep 20, and 40 evidence-grounded pilot candidates to keep up to 24 if all gates pass.
steps.step2: For arithmetic or symbolic items, sample latent programs with 2 to 4 entities, 2 to 5 numeric values, and one executable target expression. Limit values to integers or short decimals, keep prompts under 120 tokens, and reject any candidate whose solver yields more than one normalized answer.
steps.step3: For temporal items, sample normalized dates, durations, ordering relations, and inclusivity flags. Use a deterministic calendar solver and reject any candidate involving timezone assumptions, ambiguous date formats, or under-specified interval boundaries.
steps.step4: For table or list items, synthesize typed tables with 4 to 8 rows and 3 to 5 columns, then generate executable queries covering selection, filtering, counting, argmax or argmin, sorting, and one-column aggregation. Reject candidates whose edited answers become tied or non-unique after normalization.
steps.step5: For evidence-grounded items, restrict sources to dated 2025-2026 arXiv abstracts with explicit factual metadata, official versioned documentation or release notes, and dated institutional announcements with exact factual statements. Use snippets of 40 to 120 words only and require that the answer be recoverable from the snippet alone without retrieval.
steps.step6: Use the same three generation seeds [11, 22, 33] across all families. Preserve candidate counts, keep rates, and rejection reasons by seed so seed sensitivity can be reported instead of hidden.

title: Create Paraphrases, Flips, And Format Controls
description: Generate q1, q2, q3, and q4 under explicit family-specific rules so every released cluster supports deterministic recomputation and later component ablations.

steps.step1: For every seed q0, generate q1 and q2 as answer-preserving paraphrases, q3 as exactly one localized semantic flip with a changed gold answer, and q4 as a format-control rewrite that preserves the q0 semantics but changes presentation or answer format.
steps.step2: Arithmetic or symbolic q3 templates are limited to one quantity replacement, one operator swap, one comparison reversal, or one target-variable change. Recompute q3 by executing the edited latent program and reject any q3 whose normalized answer matches q0.
steps.step3: Temporal q3 templates are limited to one anchor-date edit, one interval-length edit, one inclusivity toggle, or one before-versus-after reversal. Recompute with the calendar solver and reject edits that create ambiguity or preserve the original answer.
steps.step4: Table or list q3 templates are limited to one cell-value edit, one filter predicate edit, one sort-key or sort-direction edit, or one aggregation-target edit. Recompute by executing the modified query against the modified table and reject ties or multiple valid outputs.
steps.step5: Evidence-grounded q3 templates are limited to one numeric slot replacement, one same-type entity replacement, one comparator swap, or one attribute-target swap. Edit exactly one evidence slot or one tightly localized question slot, then recompute the answer by exact span extraction or deterministic normalization from the edited snippet.
steps.step6: Generate paraphrase and flip candidates with one helper model at temperature=0.2, top_p=0.95, max_new_tokens=192, and seeds [11, 22, 33]. Keep every generated candidate until verification is complete so acceptance does not depend on one lucky draw.
steps.step7: Define q4 examples in advance, such as ISO date versus natural-language date, bare numeral versus short phrase, or one-token label versus templated sentence. Exclude q4 from all primary semantic metrics.

title: Run Automatic Checks And Human Audit Protocol
description: Use strict filtering plus dual human verification so the paper's contribution is the audited release process and the verified cluster object, not weakly checked benchmark packaging.

steps.step1: Run automatic checks on every candidate cluster: q1 and q2 preserve the normalized q0 answer, q3 changes the normalized answer, answers are unique after normalization, q3 edits are local, prompts stay under 180 tokens, and the family-specific solver or extractor reproduces the gold answer exactly.
steps.step2: Use 3 trained annotators with prior QA annotation experience and 1 adjudicator with graduate-level ML or NLP familiarity. Compensate all annotation labor at 25 USD per hour, including training, practice, production annotation, and adjudication.
steps.step3: Run a 2-hour rubric-training session followed by 20 practice clusters that will never enter the benchmark. Continue calibration until pairwise agreement reaches at least 0.80 on paraphrase validity and q3 flip validity before starting production annotation.
steps.step4: For each production candidate, collect two independent annotations over six rubric fields: paraphrase validity, flip locality, answer uniqueness, answer-change validity, outside-knowledge leakage, and fluency or well-formedness. Route every disagreement to one adjudication pass and drop unresolved items.
steps.step5: Budget human effort for roughly 144 candidates, 6 to 8 minutes per first-pass annotation, and a 15 to 20 percent adjudication rate. Save annotator IDs, timestamps, disagreement types, and final disposition for release-time auditing.
steps.step6: Apply pre-registered evidence-grounded inclusion gates on a 30-item pilot subset before admitting any evidence items to the core release: at least 20 of 30 must pass automatic uniqueness and answer-change checks, at least 18 must survive dual human verification, agreement on answer uniqueness and answer-change validity must each be at least 0.85, at least half of retained items must edit the evidence snippet itself, and fewer than 10 percent of retained items may be flagged as borderline due to normalization brittleness.
steps.step7: If any evidence-grounded gate fails, freeze the main benchmark at the 72-cluster procedural core and release the evidence-grounded family only as a pilot appendix. Do not backfill those slots with weaker evidence items.
steps.step8: Log candidate-to-kept rates, rejection reasons, annotator disagreement rates, adjudication frequency, and agreement coefficients by family, split, and seed.

title: Run Fair Baselines That Mirror Prior Benchmark Styles
description: Evaluate simple and strong baselines that map cleanly onto existing benchmark styles such as canonical QA, paraphrase-only robustness, and update-only scoring, so TwinBench can be positioned as a benchmark-engineering contribution rather than a novelty claim.

steps.step1: Evaluate four local open instruction-tuned models sequentially on the single GPU: Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and Gemma-2-9B-it. Keep the model set small enough that all evaluation, analysis, and plotting fit inside the 8-hour budget.
steps.step2: Baseline 1, simple canonical QA: q0 Seed Accuracy with family-specific normalization. Report accuracy overall, by family, and by construction split, along with mean latency, p95 latency, and peak VRAM.
steps.step3: Baseline 2, strong uncoupled benchmark baseline: Mean Semantic Variant Accuracy over q0-q3 scored independently and averaged at the prompt level, ignoring within-cluster consistency. This is the strongest non-coupled baseline because it uses all semantic prompts while discarding the cluster constraint.
steps.step4: Baseline 3, RoParQ-style paraphrase-only baseline: Paraphrase Invariance Rate over q0-q2, requiring all three answers to be correct and normalized-identical.
steps.step5: Baseline 4, CounterBench or MQuAKE-style update-only baseline: Boundary Flip Accuracy or Update-Only Accuracy on q3 alone, reported overall and separately for the evidence-grounded slice if that slice is pilot-only.
steps.step6: Baseline 5, format-sensitivity baseline: Format Control Accuracy on q4 alone, reported separately to show whether format brittleness is materially different from semantic brittleness.
steps.step7: Use identical prompt wrappers, decoding parameters, batching policy, answer normalization, and evaluation order for every model and baseline so differences come from the scoring protocol rather than hidden setup changes.

title: Measure Coupled Cluster Robustness With TwinBench Scoring
description: Run the main TwinBench evaluation to test whether models stay correct under answer-preserving paraphrases while also updating correctly under one minimal semantic flip on the same seed item.

steps.step1: Compute Paraphrase Invariance Rate (PIR): the fraction of clusters where q0, q1, and q2 are all correct and share the same normalized answer.
steps.step2: Compute Boundary Flip Accuracy (BFA): the fraction of clusters where q3 is answered correctly under the deterministically recomputed q3 gold answer.
steps.step3: Compute Cluster Semantic Score (CSS), the primary metric: a cluster passes only when q0, q1, and q2 are all correct and mutually consistent and q3 is also correct with a normalized answer different from the paraphrase answer.
steps.step4: Compute Format Control Accuracy (FCA) on q4 only and keep it out of the primary score so formatting sensitivity cannot inflate or deflate the semantic headline metric.
steps.step5: Report q0 Accuracy, Mean Semantic Variant Accuracy, PIR, BFA, CSS, and FCA overall, by family, by construction split, and by q3 flip template. Clearly label the evidence-grounded slice as core or pilot depending on whether its gates were passed.
steps.step6: Use 1000 paired bootstrap resamples over clusters for every model and metric, with one shared bootstrap seed across all models. Report 95 percent confidence intervals and paired deltas for q0 Accuracy minus CSS, Mean Semantic Variant Accuracy minus CSS, PIR minus CSS, and BFA minus CSS.

title: Ablate Every Claimed Benchmark Contribution
description: Test whether the observed signal comes from the benchmark's claimed contributions: the verified cluster release unit, coupled scoring, deterministic recomputation, and audit protocol.

steps.step1: Ablation A, remove paraphrase coupling: score q0 plus q3 only and compare with CSS. This isolates the value of requiring paraphrase invariance in the same cluster.
steps.step2: Ablation B, remove answer-updating pressure: score q0, q1, and q2 only and compare with CSS. This isolates the value of the answer-changing flip.
steps.step3: Ablation C, include q4 in the main score and compare with CSS. This tests whether format sensitivity should remain separated from the semantic headline metric.
steps.step4: Ablation D, use automatic filtering only on a held-out candidate subset and compare with the final dual-verified release. Measure ambiguity rate, score variance, and model-ranking stability to quantify the value of human auditing.
steps.step5: Ablation E, disable deterministic recomputation on a held-out validation subset by replacing exact solver or extractor checks with raw string labels. Measure label disagreement and score instability to test whether deterministic gold recomputation matters.
steps.step6: Ablation F, relax the evidence-grounded source policy on a non-release validation pool by allowing broader sources or less-local edits. Measure failures in answer uniqueness, extractability, and fluency to justify the stricter evidence gates.
steps.step7: Ablation G, exact-string-only scoring: recompute all main metrics without normalization and compare with normalized scoring to ensure results are not driven by overly permissive normalization.
steps.step8: For every ablation, report per-model deltas, 95 percent paired bootstrap confidence intervals, and whether the effect replicates in both construction splits.

title: Assess Statistical Power, Success Criteria, And Budget Fit
description: Define success, failure, and practical feasibility in advance so the final paper can make narrow, defensible claims about benchmark quality and diagnostic value.

steps.step1: Primary success criterion: average CSS must be at least 8 percentage points below average q0 Seed Accuracy across the four evaluated models, and the paired q0-minus-CSS gap must exclude zero for at least three of the four models under bootstrap confidence intervals.
steps.step2: Secondary success criterion: CSS must be at least 5 percentage points below the strong uncoupled baseline of Mean Semantic Variant Accuracy on average, showing that coupled scoring adds diagnostic signal beyond item-level averaging.
steps.step3: Evidence-slice success criterion: if the evidence-grounded family enters the core release, CSS on that family must be at least 5 percentage points below q3 Update-Only Accuracy on average. If the evidence slice is pilot-only, report this comparison as exploratory diagnostic analysis only.
steps.step4: Benchmark-quality success criterion: overall dual-annotator agreement must exceed 0.80 on paraphrase validity and flip locality, and evidence-grounded agreement on answer uniqueness and answer-change validity must exceed 0.85 if that family enters the core release.
steps.step5: Split-stability success criterion: Split A and Split B must show the same direction of q0-minus-CSS gap for at least three of the four models, and split-wise CSS rankings must have Spearman correlation at least 0.7. Treat this as a stability check, not a leaderboard claim.
steps.step6: Power-analysis step: before interpreting any rank differences, estimate the minimum detectable paired difference in CSS under 1000 cluster bootstraps using the final cluster count. If the detectable effect exceeds the observed pairwise model gaps, explicitly mark rank differences as underpowered and avoid strong separation claims.
steps.step7: Falsification conditions: consider the benchmark claim unsupported if CSS stays within 5 points of q0 Accuracy for all models, if the q0-minus-CSS gap fails to replicate across splits, if human agreement misses the pre-registered thresholds, or if the procedural core alone shows no consistent diagnostic gap after the evidence slice is dropped.
steps.step8: Runtime estimate under one GPU: environment setup and smoke tests 0.5 hour; candidate generation and solver checks 0.75 hour; paraphrase and flip generation plus automatic filtering 1.0 to 1.25 hours; four-model inference over 96 clusters x 5 prompts = 480 prompts per model, roughly 2.0 to 2.75 GPU hours total depending on batching; CPU-only bootstrap, agreement analysis, and split statistics under 1 hour; figure and table generation 0.5 hour. Total projected compute is about 4.75 to 6.0 hours plus annotation time, which fits under the 8-hour experiment budget.
steps.step9: Parallelization plan: GPU runs are sequential because there is one GPU. Overlap each GPU batch with CPU-only scoring, bootstrap preparation, agreement aggregation, and plot rendering on the 4 CPU cores whenever intermediate outputs are ready.

title: Generate Benchmark-Quality And Failure-Analysis Figures
description: Produce paper-ready tables and plots that emphasize construction quality, uncertainty, and diagnostic failure modes rather than superficial leaderboard movement.

steps.step1: Create Table 1 with benchmark construction statistics: candidate counts, kept counts, rejection rates, flip-template counts, source-type counts, annotator agreement, adjudication rates, and seed-specific keep rates by family and by construction split.
steps.step2: Create Table 2 with per-model q0 Accuracy, Mean Semantic Variant Accuracy, PIR, BFA, CSS, FCA, overall latency, p95 latency, and peak VRAM. Add 95 percent confidence intervals for every accuracy-like metric.
steps.step3: Create Table 3 with ablation deltas for removing paraphrase coupling, removing answer-updating pressure, adding q4 to the main score, removing human verification, removing deterministic recomputation, relaxing evidence rules, and removing normalization.
steps.step4: Create Figure 1 as grouped bars for q0 Accuracy, Mean Semantic Variant Accuracy, PIR, BFA, and CSS by model to visualize how coupled scoring lowers measured robustness relative to easier baselines.
steps.step5: Create Figure 2 as a split-wise diagnostic plot showing q0-minus-CSS gaps for Split A and Split B with bootstrap confidence intervals, so stability is visible without overclaiming rank changes.
steps.step6: Create Figure 3 as a family-level stacked breakdown of failure modes: paraphrase failures, flip-update failures, combined failures, and format-only failures.
steps.step7: Create Figure 4 as a candidate-to-kept waterfall for each family, highlighting where evidence-grounded items fail automatic checks, human verification, or adjudication.
steps.step8: Prepare an appendix package with 8 to 12 qualitative cluster case studies, one fully specified JSON cluster example with audit metadata, one table separating compute time from annotation time, and one short related-work comparison table mapping TwinBench metrics to RoParQ-style, CounterBench-style, MQuAKE-style, LastingBench-style, and LGIP-adjacent evaluation views.
